questions.prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
A Cooler Block
On summer afternoons, the difference between two city blocks can feel larger than the distance between them. One block may have porches shaded by maples, while another has parking lots, brick walls, and very little shade. A neighborhood group in East Harbor wanted to understand why some streets stayed noticeably cooler than others. With help from a local college, volunteers measured sidewalk temperatures on twelve blocks at 3:00 p.m. during a July heat wave.
The group found that blocks with mature trees averaged 7 degrees cooler than blocks with little shade. However, the report did not recommend planting trees as the only solution. Young trees take years to provide significant shade, and several newly planted trees in the neighborhood had died during dry summers. The report recommended planting trees near bus stops and apartment entrances first, adding watering bags for the first three years, and asking nearby residents or businesses to help monitor the trees.
The group also noticed that shaded blocks encouraged more outdoor activity. On one cooler street, residents sat outside, children walked to a corner store, and older adults used benches near the bus stop. On the hottest blocks, people hurried indoors or waited for buses in narrow strips of shade.
The report’s main point was not that trees solve every heat problem. Instead, it argued that targeted planting, maintenance, and community participation can make public spaces more comfortable and safer during extreme heat.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Mature trees can improve neighborhood comfort, but tree planting works best when it is targeted and maintained.
choices.B: City residents should avoid going outside during July heat waves whenever possible.
choices.C: East Harbor should replace all parking lots with parks and benches.
choices.D: Volunteers can measure sidewalk temperatures more accurately than city officials.

questions.correctAnswerText: Mature trees can improve neighborhood comfort, but tree planting works best when it is targeted and maintained.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that trees reduce heat, but it emphasizes targeted planting, maintenance, and community participation.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “A Cooler Block.”
Which detail best supports the idea that trees alone are not an immediate solution?

choices.A: Volunteers measured sidewalk temperatures at 3:00 p.m.
choices.B: Blocks with mature trees averaged 7 degrees cooler.
choices.C: Young trees take years to provide significant shade.
choices.D: Residents sat outside on one cooler street.

questions.correctAnswerText: Young trees take years to provide significant shade.
questions.explanation: This detail shows why planting trees is useful but does not solve heat problems immediately.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “A Cooler Block.”
Which TWO recommendations did the report make?

choices.A: Measure sidewalk temperatures during every season.
choices.B: Plant trees first near bus stops and apartment entrances.
choices.C: Remove benches from the hottest blocks.
choices.D: Use watering bags for the first three years.
choices.E: Require every resident to plant a tree in front of a home.

questions.correctAnswerText: Plant trees first near bus stops and apartment entrances. | Use watering bags for the first three years.
questions.explanation: The passage specifically recommends prioritizing bus stops and apartment entrances and using watering bags for three years.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “A Cooler Block.”
What can be inferred from the description of people on the hottest blocks?

choices.A: People on those blocks disliked outdoor activities in every season.
choices.B: Heat affected how long people were willing to remain outside.
choices.C: Bus service was less frequent on the hottest blocks.
choices.D: Residents on those blocks did not understand the value of trees.

questions.correctAnswerText: Heat affected how long people were willing to remain outside.
questions.explanation: The contrast between cooler and hotter blocks suggests that heat influenced people’s outdoor behavior.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “A Cooler Block.”
In the sentence “trees can help buffer residents from extreme heat,” the word buffer most nearly means [D1].
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=protect
questions.explanation: In context, buffer means to protect or reduce the impact of extreme heat.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “A Cooler Block.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Newly planted trees had died during dry summers.
tiles.B: Residents sat outside and children walked to a corner store.
tiles.C: Volunteers measured sidewalk temperatures on twelve blocks.
tiles.D: The report recommended removing all brick walls.

targets.T2: Evidence that cooler streets may encourage outdoor activity
targets.T3: Evidence that the study used local data

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Newly planted trees had died during dry summers.; T2=Residents sat outside and children walked to a corner store.; T3=Volunteers measured sidewalk temperatures on twelve blocks.
questions.explanation: Each tile provides evidence for the matching target.

questions.prompt:
Read the workplace document and answer the question.
Emergency Notification Drill Notice
To: All warehouse and office employees
From: Safety Coordinator
Subject: Emergency Notification Drill — Thursday
On Thursday at 10:15 a.m., the company will conduct a test of its emergency notification system. Employees will receive a text message, an email, and an automated phone call. The message will clearly state that it is a drill.
No evacuation is required. Employees should remain at their workstations unless they are already scheduled for a break or meeting. When you receive the test message, reply YES to the text message within five minutes. Employees without text access should confirm receipt by clicking the link in the email.
The safety team will use the results to identify incorrect phone numbers, inactive email addresses, and areas of the building where notifications are delayed. If you recently changed your phone number or email address, update your contact information in the employee portal by noon on Friday.
This drill is not a performance review. Individual response times will not be used for discipline. However, participation is important. In an actual emergency, delayed contact could leave workers without critical instructions.
Supervisors should remind team members of the drill during Thursday’s morning huddle. Questions may be directed to the safety coordinator before the test begins.
What is the main purpose of the notice?

choices.A: To announce a required evacuation from the warehouse
choices.B: To explain how employees should respond to a test of the emergency notification system
choices.C: To warn employees that slow responses will result in discipline
choices.D: To introduce a new employee performance review system

questions.correctAnswerText: To explain how employees should respond to a test of the emergency notification system
questions.explanation: The notice gives instructions for a drill and explains how employees should confirm receipt.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Emergency Notification Drill Notice.”
What should employees do if they recently changed their contact information?

choices.A: Wait until after the drill to contact a supervisor
choices.B: Reply YES to every message they receive
choices.C: Update the employee portal by noon on Friday
choices.D: Leave the building at 10:15 a.m.

questions.correctAnswerText: Update the employee portal by noon on Friday
questions.explanation: The notice tells employees with changed contact information to update the portal by noon on Friday.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Emergency Notification Drill Notice.”
Which TWO statements are supported by the notice?

choices.A: The drill message will clearly state that it is only a drill.
choices.B: Employees must gather outside the building during the test.
choices.C: Individual response times will be used to decide promotions.
choices.D: Employees who do not have text access may confirm through email.
choices.E: The drill will begin at noon on Friday.

questions.correctAnswerText: The drill message will clearly state that it is only a drill. | Employees who do not have text access may confirm through email.
questions.explanation: Both statements are directly supported by the notice.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Emergency Notification Drill Notice.”
Why will the safety team review the results of the drill?

choices.A: To decide which employees should receive a bonus
choices.B: To find problems with contact information or notification delays
choices.C: To determine whether employees prefer email or phone calls
choices.D: To schedule future staff meetings

questions.correctAnswerText: To find problems with contact information or notification delays
questions.explanation: The safety team will use results to identify incorrect contact information and notification delays.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Emergency Notification Drill Notice.”
Which sentence provides the strongest reason employees should participate seriously in the drill?

choices.A: “The message will clearly state that it is a drill.”
choices.B: “Employees should remain at their workstations unless they are already scheduled for a break or meeting.”
choices.C: “This drill is not a performance review.”
choices.D: “In an actual emergency, delayed contact could leave workers without critical instructions.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “In an actual emergency, delayed contact could leave workers without critical instructions.”
questions.explanation: This sentence explains the real safety reason for participating in the drill.

questions.prompt:
Read the argument and answer the question.
Short Certificates, Long-Term Goals
Community colleges should offer short-term career certificates that can also count toward longer degree programs. Some people worry that these certificates may distract students from completing associate degrees. That concern is understandable, but it overlooks how many students actually attend community college. Many work full time, support families, or return to school after years away. For these students, a two-year degree may be the goal, but it may not be possible all at once.
A short certificate can provide a useful first step. For example, a student studying medical office administration might earn a billing certificate after one semester, qualify for a better job, and then return for additional courses that apply toward a degree. Local employers also benefit when certificate programs are designed with real skill needs in mind. In a recent survey by the county workforce board, more than half of health-care employers reported difficulty finding applicants with basic records-management training.
Certificates should not replace degrees. Colleges must be careful to show students how each certificate fits into a larger pathway. Advisers should explain which credits transfer, what jobs the certificate may support, and what additional education may be needed. When designed responsibly, short-term certificates can help students make progress instead of forcing them to choose between school and immediate financial needs.
What is the author’s main claim?

choices.A: Community colleges should stop offering associate degrees.
choices.B: Short-term certificates should be offered when they connect to longer education and career pathways.
choices.C: Health-care employers should provide all job training after hiring workers.
choices.D: Students with full-time jobs should not enroll in college programs.

questions.correctAnswerText: Short-term certificates should be offered when they connect to longer education and career pathways.
questions.explanation: The author supports certificates when they are designed responsibly and connected to degree pathways.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Short Certificates, Long-Term Goals.”
Which evidence best supports the claim that certificates can meet workforce needs?

choices.A: Some students return to school after years away.
choices.B: Advisers should explain which credits transfer.
choices.C: More than half of local health-care employers reported difficulty finding applicants with records-management training.
choices.D: Certificates should not replace degrees.

questions.correctAnswerText: More than half of local health-care employers reported difficulty finding applicants with records-management training.
questions.explanation: This evidence directly shows a workforce need that certificates could address.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Short Certificates, Long-Term Goals.”
Which TWO points does the author acknowledge to make the argument more balanced?

choices.A: Some people worry certificates may distract students from completing degrees.
choices.B: All employers prefer certificates to college degrees.
choices.C: Certificates should not replace degrees.
choices.D: Students never return to school after earning a certificate.
choices.E: Colleges should avoid advising students about career pathways.

questions.correctAnswerText: Some people worry certificates may distract students from completing degrees. | Certificates should not replace degrees.
questions.explanation: The author acknowledges these concerns to present a balanced argument.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Short Certificates, Long-Term Goals.”
The author’s argument depends most on which assumption?

choices.A: Every student can complete a two-year degree without interruption.
choices.B: Certificate credits can be planned so they help students continue toward a degree.
choices.C: Employers should stop using interviews when hiring new workers.
choices.D: Community colleges should focus only on health-care programs.

questions.correctAnswerText: Certificate credits can be planned so they help students continue toward a degree.
questions.explanation: The argument depends on certificates fitting into a larger educational pathway.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Short Certificates, Long-Term Goals.”
Choose the sentence that would best strengthen the author’s argument if added after paragraph 2: [D1]

D1: A college in a neighboring county reported that 62 percent of certificate earners later enrolled in the next level of the same program.
Many students enjoy meeting classmates who have similar schedules.
College buildings should remain open later in the evening.

questions.correct: D1=A college in a neighboring county reported that 62 percent of certificate earners later enrolled in the next level of the same program.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=A college in a neighboring county reported that 62 percent of certificate earners later enrolled in the next level of the same program.
questions.explanation: This sentence provides relevant evidence that certificate earners may continue their education.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Short Certificates, Long-Term Goals.”
Which statement best describes the author’s response to the counterclaim that certificates may distract students from degrees?

choices.A: The author ignores the concern and discusses only employer needs.
choices.B: The author agrees that certificates should replace degrees entirely.
choices.C: The author admits the concern but argues that careful program design can make certificates part of a degree pathway.
choices.D: The author says that students should avoid certificates unless they already have a degree.

questions.correctAnswerText: The author admits the concern but argues that careful program design can make certificates part of a degree pathway.
questions.explanation: The author recognizes the counterclaim and answers it with a design-based solution.

questions.prompt:
Read the literary excerpt and answer the question.
The First Loaf
Mara had worked the bakery counter for three months before Mr. Ellis let her shape the morning loaves. Until then, she had measured coffee, wrapped pastries, and learned the names of customers who arrived before sunrise. She had also watched Mr. Ellis turn dough with a motion that looked effortless, as if the dough had already decided where to fold.
On Tuesday, he placed a metal bowl in front of her. “This batch is yours,” he said.
Mara pressed her palms into the dough. It stuck to her fingers and pulled unevenly across the table. She glanced at the clock. The first customers would arrive in forty minutes. Mr. Ellis did not take over. He only dusted the table with flour and said, “Listen with your hands. If you force it, it tears.”
Mara tried again, slower this time. She felt the dough stretch before it resisted. She folded it once, then again. The surface became smoother. When the loaves came from the oven, one had split along the side, but the others were round and golden.
At 6:10, Mrs. Alvarez entered for her usual loaf. Mara reached automatically for one Mr. Ellis had shaped. Then she stopped and picked up one of her own. “This one is fresh,” she said.
Mrs. Alvarez smiled. “That’s why I come early.”
Mara watched her carry the loaf into the gray morning. The split loaf still sat on the cooling rack, but she no longer saw it as proof that she had failed. It was simply one loaf, and tomorrow there would be another batch.
What is the main conflict in the excerpt?

choices.A: Mara must decide whether to leave her job at the bakery.
choices.B: Mara struggles to perform a new skill while learning to trust her own progress.
choices.C: Mr. Ellis refuses to teach Mara how to work with customers.
choices.D: Mrs. Alvarez complains about the quality of the bread.

questions.correctAnswerText: Mara struggles to perform a new skill while learning to trust her own progress.
questions.explanation: The excerpt focuses on Mara’s attempt to shape bread and her shift from doubt to confidence.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The First Loaf.”
Why does Mr. Ellis most likely refuse to take over when Mara struggles?

choices.A: He wants Mara to learn through guided practice.
choices.B: He is too busy serving customers.
choices.C: He does not care whether the bread turns out well.
choices.D: He wants to punish Mara for working slowly.

questions.correctAnswerText: He wants Mara to learn through guided practice.
questions.explanation: Mr. Ellis gives advice instead of taking over, which allows Mara to learn by doing.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The First Loaf.”
What does Mara’s decision to give Mrs. Alvarez one of her own loaves show?

choices.A: Mara wants to hide the bread Mr. Ellis shaped.
choices.B: Mara has gained enough confidence to share her work.
choices.C: Mara thinks Mrs. Alvarez will not notice the difference.
choices.D: Mara is trying to finish her shift early.

questions.correctAnswerText: Mara has gained enough confidence to share her work.
questions.explanation: Choosing one of her own loaves shows that Mara is beginning to trust her ability.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “The First Loaf.”
Which TWO details show Mara’s changing attitude?

choices.A: She watches Mr. Ellis shape dough before she is allowed to try.
choices.B: She first reaches for a loaf Mr. Ellis shaped.
choices.C: She stops and chooses one of her own loaves for Mrs. Alvarez.
choices.D: She sees the split loaf as “simply one loaf” rather than proof of failure.
choices.E: She measures coffee and wraps pastries during earlier shifts.

questions.correctAnswerText: She stops and chooses one of her own loaves for Mrs. Alvarez. | She sees the split loaf as “simply one loaf” rather than proof of failure.
questions.explanation: These details show Mara’s movement from uncertainty toward confidence and resilience.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The First Loaf.”
In the sentence “She felt the dough stretch before it resisted,” the word resisted most nearly means

choices.A: became easier to lift.
choices.B: pushed back against pressure.
choices.C: changed color quickly.
choices.D: made a loud sound.

questions.correctAnswerText: pushed back against pressure.
questions.explanation: In context, the dough “resisted” when it became less willing to stretch.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The First Loaf.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Mr. Ellis tells Mara, “Listen with your hands.”
tiles.B: One loaf splits along the side.
tiles.C: Mara gives Mrs. Alvarez one of her own loaves.
tiles.D: Mrs. Alvarez asks for a refund.

targets.T2: A result that is imperfect but not disastrous
targets.T3: A sign of Mara’s growing confidence

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Mr. Ellis tells Mara, “Listen with your hands.”; T2=One loaf splits along the side.; T3=Mara gives Mrs. Alvarez one of her own loaves.
questions.explanation: The events match instruction, imperfection, and confidence.

questions.prompt:
Read the science communication passage and answer the question.
Sleep, Study, and Memory
Students often hear that they should not “cram” before a test, but the reason is not simply that cramming feels stressful. Memory researchers have found that sleep plays an important role in strengthening new learning. In one small study, adults learned pairs of unfamiliar words in the evening. Half the group slept overnight before being tested, while the other half stayed awake for the same number of hours. The group that slept remembered more word pairs the next morning.
The researchers did not claim that sleep magically creates knowledge. Participants still had to study the words first. Sleep seemed to help the brain organize and stabilize information that had already been introduced. Other studies suggest that review spaced across several days is usually more effective than one long session because each review gives the brain another chance to retrieve and strengthen the memory.
The practical lesson is not that students should study less. It is that study time should be planned. A student who reviews vocabulary for twenty minutes on three different evenings may remember more than a student who studies for one hour only the night before a test. Sleep, breaks, and repeated retrieval can work together.
However, the passage of time alone is not enough. Re-reading notes without checking understanding may feel familiar without producing strong recall. Practice questions, explaining ideas aloud, and writing brief summaries are more active ways to test what has been learned.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Students should stop studying once they feel tired.
choices.B: Sleep and spaced review can strengthen learning when combined with active study.
choices.C: Cramming is effective because it keeps information fresh.
choices.D: Memory researchers disagree about whether studying is useful.

questions.correctAnswerText: Sleep and spaced review can strengthen learning when combined with active study.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that sleep, repeated review, and active recall support memory.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Sleep, Study, and Memory.”
In the study described, what did the group that slept do better than the group that stayed awake?

choices.A: They learned more languages.
choices.B: They remembered more word pairs the next morning.
choices.C: They studied for a longer total time.
choices.D: They avoided taking a test.

questions.correctAnswerText: They remembered more word pairs the next morning.
questions.explanation: The passage states that the sleeping group remembered more word pairs.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Sleep, Study, and Memory.”
Which inference is best supported by the passage?

choices.A: A student who never studies can still perform well after sleeping.
choices.B: Active recall is more useful than simply looking over notes.
choices.C: Studying in the morning is always better than studying at night.
choices.D: One long study session is the only reliable way to prepare.

questions.correctAnswerText: Active recall is more useful than simply looking over notes.
questions.explanation: The passage says re-reading may feel familiar without producing recall and recommends practice questions, explaining, and summaries.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Sleep, Study, and Memory.”
How is the passage mainly organized?

choices.A: It presents a research finding, explains its meaning, and offers practical study advice.
choices.B: It lists the history of memory research from oldest to newest.
choices.C: It compares two schools that use different testing schedules.
choices.D: It tells a personal story about one student’s exam experience.

questions.correctAnswerText: It presents a research finding, explains its meaning, and offers practical study advice.
questions.explanation: The passage begins with research, explains the role of sleep and review, and ends with study strategies.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Sleep, Study, and Memory.”
Which sentence best supports the idea that repeated review helps memory?

choices.A: “Students often hear that they should not ‘cram’ before a test.”
choices.B: “Participants still had to study the words first.”
choices.C: “Other studies suggest that review spaced across several days is usually more effective than one long session.”
choices.D: “The passage of time alone is not enough.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Other studies suggest that review spaced across several days is usually more effective than one long session.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly supports the idea that repeated review helps memory.

questions.prompt:
Read the editing passage and choose the best words for the blanks.
Community Garden Tool Library
(1) The Maple Street Community Garden will open a small tool library this spring. (2) Garden members may borrow rakes, gloves, hand trowels, and watering cans for up to three days. (3) The project is designed for residents who want to garden but do not have space to store equipment at home. (4) [D1], it may help reduce waste because fewer households will need to buy tools they use only a few times each year. (5) Each borrowed item [D2] cleaned and returned to the storage shed by Sunday evening. (6) Members who return damaged tools should leave a note so volunteers can repair or replace them.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=In addition; D2=must be
questions.explanation: “In addition” correctly adds another benefit, and “must be cleaned” is grammatically correct.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Community Garden Tool Library.”
Which revision of sentence 2 is most concise while keeping the meaning?

choices.A: Garden members may borrow rakes, gloves, hand trowels, and watering cans for up to three days.
choices.B: Garden members, who are members of the garden, may borrow tools for a period of time lasting up to three days.
choices.C: Garden members may borrow rakes, gloves, hand trowels, watering cans, and other things for a total amount of time.
choices.D: Garden members may borrow tools because borrowing tools is something members can do.

questions.correctAnswerText: Garden members may borrow rakes, gloves, hand trowels, and watering cans for up to three days.
questions.explanation: This version is clear and concise without unnecessary repetition.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses correct subject-verb agreement?

choices.A: The boxes of donated gloves is stored in the shed.
choices.B: The list of available tools are posted near the gate.
choices.C: The volunteers who manage the tool library arrive every Saturday.
choices.D: The schedule for weekend repairs have changed.

questions.correctAnswerText: The volunteers who manage the tool library arrive every Saturday.
questions.explanation: The plural subject “volunteers” correctly takes the plural verb “arrive.”

questions.prompt: Which revision best improves pronoun clarity?

choices.A: When Lena gave the form to Priya, she signed it immediately.
choices.B: Lena gave the form to Priya, and Priya signed it immediately.
choices.C: She signed it immediately after Lena gave the form to Priya.
choices.D: When it was given to Priya by Lena, she signed it immediately.

questions.correctAnswerText: Lena gave the form to Priya, and Priya signed it immediately.
questions.explanation: Repeating “Priya” makes clear who signed the form.

questions.prompt:
Choose the best transition for the blank.
The library added evening computer classes for adults. [D1], attendance increased among residents who work during the day.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=As a result
questions.explanation: The second sentence shows a result of adding evening classes.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?

choices.A: Before the meeting begins please silence your phone.
choices.B: Before the meeting begins, please silence your phone.
choices.C: Before, the meeting begins please silence your phone.
choices.D: Before the meeting, begins please silence your phone.

questions.correctAnswerText: Before the meeting begins, please silence your phone.
questions.explanation: The introductory dependent clause should be followed by a comma.

questions.prompt:
Which revision best combines the sentences?
The class met on Tuesday. The class reviewed interview skills.

choices.A: The class met on Tuesday, and the class reviewed interview skills.
choices.B: Meeting on Tuesday and reviewing interview skills was the class.
choices.C: On Tuesday, the class reviewed interview skills.
choices.D: The class, which was a class, met Tuesday for interview skills that were reviewed.

questions.correctAnswerText: On Tuesday, the class reviewed interview skills.
questions.explanation: This choice combines the ideas clearly and concisely.

questions.prompt: Which sentence maintains consistent verb tense?

choices.A: The manager reviewed the schedule and approves the change yesterday.
choices.B: The manager reviews the schedule and approved the change yesterday.
choices.C: The manager reviewed the schedule and approved the change yesterday.
choices.D: The manager had reviewed the schedule and approves the change yesterday.

questions.correctAnswerText: The manager reviewed the schedule and approved the change yesterday.
questions.explanation: Both verbs are in the past tense, matching “yesterday.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence places the modifier correctly?

choices.A: Covered in fresh paint, the volunteers admired the benches.
choices.B: The volunteers admired the benches covered in fresh paint.
choices.C: The benches admired the volunteers covered in fresh paint.
choices.D: Covered in fresh paint, admiration came from the volunteers.

questions.correctAnswerText: The volunteers admired the benches covered in fresh paint.
questions.explanation: The modifier is placed near “benches,” the thing covered in fresh paint.

questions.prompt:
Read the paired passages and answer the question.
Passage A: Keep the Reading Room Quiet
The old reading room at Northside Library should remain a quiet study space. The room has thick walls, large tables, and reliable lighting. Students, job seekers, and adults studying for licensing exams use it because it is one of the few public places in the neighborhood where silence is expected. Last month, library staff counted an average of 38 people using the room between 4:00 p.m. and 7:00 p.m. on weekdays.
Some residents have suggested turning the room into a media lab with recording equipment and group workstations. That idea is creative, but it ignores a basic need. Many homes are crowded or noisy. A person preparing for an exam or filling out job applications may not have another quiet place to go. The library already offers group tables near the entrance and meeting rooms that can be reserved. Before changing the reading room, the library should improve the spaces that already support group projects.
Passage B: Build the Media Lab
Northside Library should convert the old reading room into a media lab for teens and adults. A modern library should help people create information, not just consume it. Residents increasingly need digital skills for school, work, and small business projects. A media lab with microphones, editing software, and group workstations would let patrons practice presentations, record community histories, and build job portfolios.
Quiet study is important, but the current reading room is not the only possible study space. The library has underused tables on the second floor, and simple changes such as signs and desk dividers could make those areas more focused. Meanwhile, the neighborhood has no free public space with media equipment. Private studios are expensive, and many residents cannot afford editing software at home. A media lab would expand opportunity for people who are currently left out of digital creation.
What is the central difference between the two passages?

choices.A: Passage A values quiet study in the room, while Passage B values using the room for digital creation.
choices.B: Passage A argues that libraries should close earlier, while Passage B argues that libraries should stay open all night.
choices.C: Passage A supports private studios, while Passage B opposes all library technology.
choices.D: Passage A focuses on children’s books, while Passage B focuses on library fines.

questions.correctAnswerText: Passage A values quiet study in the room, while Passage B values using the room for digital creation.
questions.explanation: The two passages disagree about the best use of the library room.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about Northside Library.
Which piece of evidence most directly supports Passage A’s argument?

choices.A: The reading room has thick walls, large tables, and reliable lighting.
choices.B: A modern library should help people create information.
choices.C: Private studios are expensive.
choices.D: Residents can record community histories.

questions.correctAnswerText: The reading room has thick walls, large tables, and reliable lighting.
questions.explanation: This evidence supports Passage A’s argument that the room is well suited for quiet study.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to the paired passages about Northside Library.
Which TWO claims from Passage B are supported with reasons or evidence?

choices.A: A media lab would help residents practice presentations and build job portfolios.
choices.B: The reading room should be converted because silence is never useful.
choices.C: Private studios are expensive, making free public equipment valuable.
choices.D: Library patrons are no longer interested in reading printed books.
choices.E: Every library meeting room should be permanently closed.

questions.correctAnswerText: A media lab would help residents practice presentations and build job portfolios. | Private studios are expensive, making free public equipment valuable.
questions.explanation: Passage B supports these claims with examples and reasoning.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about Northside Library.
Which statement from Passage A is most vulnerable to challenge by Passage B?

choices.A: “The room has thick walls, large tables, and reliable lighting.”
choices.B: “Students, job seekers, and adults studying for licensing exams use it.”
choices.C: “The library already offers group tables near the entrance and meeting rooms that can be reserved.”
choices.D: “Before changing the reading room, the library should improve the spaces that already support group projects.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Before changing the reading room, the library should improve the spaces that already support group projects.”
questions.explanation: Passage B challenges this by arguing that the neighborhood lacks free public media equipment.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about Northside Library.
Which counterclaim would Passage A’s author most likely make against Passage B?

choices.A: Digital skills are never useful for work or school.
choices.B: A media lab may be useful, but it should not replace one of the neighborhood’s few reliable quiet spaces.
choices.C: Libraries should offer only printed books and no other services.
choices.D: Residents who need recording equipment should stop using the library.

questions.correctAnswerText: A media lab may be useful, but it should not replace one of the neighborhood’s few reliable quiet spaces.
questions.explanation: Passage A recognizes other library uses but prioritizes preserving quiet study space.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about Northside Library.
Which statement is a supported inference from both passages?

choices.A: Northside Library has unlimited space for every possible program.
choices.B: The library must choose how to use limited space to meet different community needs.
choices.C: No residents currently use the library after 4:00 p.m.
choices.D: The media lab has already been built on the second floor.

questions.correctAnswerText: The library must choose how to use limited space to meet different community needs.
questions.explanation: Both passages address competing needs for the same library room.

questions.prompt:
Which revision fixes the misplaced modifier?
Walking to the bus stop, the rain soaked Jordan’s backpack.

choices.A: Walking to the bus stop, Jordan’s backpack was soaked by the rain.
choices.B: The rain, walking to the bus stop, soaked Jordan’s backpack.
choices.C: As Jordan walked to the bus stop, the rain soaked his backpack.
choices.D: Jordan’s backpack walked to the bus stop and was soaked by the rain.

questions.correctAnswerText: As Jordan walked to the bus stop, the rain soaked his backpack.
questions.explanation: This revision makes clear that Jordan, not the rain or backpack, was walking.

questions.prompt:
Which choice best combines the sentences without changing their meaning?
Nora completed the application. She submitted it before the deadline.

choices.A: Nora completed the application and submitted it before the deadline.
choices.B: Nora completed the application, it submitted before the deadline.
choices.C: Before the deadline was submitted, Nora completed the application.
choices.D: Nora completed, and the application submitted it before the deadline.

questions.correctAnswerText: Nora completed the application and submitted it before the deadline.
questions.explanation: This sentence combines the two actions clearly and grammatically.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses commas correctly?

choices.A: The workshop, which begins at six, will cover resume writing.
choices.B: The workshop which begins, at six will cover resume writing.
choices.C: The workshop which, begins at six, will cover resume writing.
choices.D: The workshop, which begins at six will cover, resume writing.

questions.correctAnswerText: The workshop, which begins at six, will cover resume writing.
questions.explanation: The nonessential clause “which begins at six” is correctly set off by commas.

questions.prompt:
Read both passages. Write an evidence-based essay of 300–500 words explaining which position is better supported. Use evidence from both passages. Maintain a formal tone and organize your response clearly.
Passage A:
School Streets Should Be Safer Before They Are Faster
The city should install traffic-calming features near elementary and middle schools. These features may include raised crosswalks, curb extensions, clearer signs, and lower speed limits during arrival and dismissal times. Some drivers worry that these changes will add a few minutes to their commute, but student safety should be the priority on streets where children walk every day.
A recent count by parent volunteers found that more than 200 students cross Pine Avenue during the morning drop-off period. The same count recorded 47 drivers traveling above the posted speed limit in just one hour. Children are still developing judgment about distance and speed, and even careful students can make mistakes when traffic moves quickly.
Traffic calming does not have to block access. Emergency vehicles can still use school streets, and delivery drivers can plan around clearly posted school-hour limits. Other cities have used raised crosswalks and curb extensions to reduce speeding without closing streets entirely. The goal is not to punish drivers. The goal is to design streets so that a small mistake does not become a serious injury.
Passage B:
Keep School Traffic Moving
The city should not add traffic-calming features around schools until it studies the effect on traffic flow. Families, workers, delivery drivers, and emergency responders all use the same streets. Slowing traffic too much may create backups that increase frustration and encourage risky behavior, such as sudden turns onto side streets.
Supporters of traffic calming often focus on safety, but safety can improve in other ways. Schools can assign staff to guide crossings, teach students safer walking habits, and ask police to monitor speeding during the first month of school. These steps address dangerous behavior without permanently changing the roads.
The city also needs better data before spending money on construction. A volunteer count may show that some drivers speed, but it does not prove which design would solve the problem. Installing raised crosswalks and curb extensions could cost more than signs, education, or targeted enforcement. Before changing school streets, officials should collect traffic data, compare options, and choose the solution that protects students while keeping the neighborhood moving.
Writing Task:
Which passage presents the better-supported argument about whether the city should install traffic-calming features near schools? Write an essay explaining your position. Use evidence from both passages to support your analysis.
Rubric Guidance:
• Analyze which argument is better supported.
• Use relevant evidence from both passages.
• Explain why the evidence supports your claim.
• Address weaknesses or limitations in the less-supported argument.
• Organize ideas logically.
• Use clear sentence structure, grammar, and formal language.
questions.correctAnswerText: Not auto-scored
questions.explanation: Extended Response answers should be reviewed using the rubric guidance. Strong responses make a clear claim, use evidence from both passages, explain why one argument is better supported, and maintain formal organization and language.
